html:
Runestone Sö 189, made of granite, is 2.3 meters high and 0.7-0.5 meters wide. The inscription faces southeast and northwest. The inscription on the right side of the stone is difficult to interpret and has not been translated below.

"Onese raised the stone in memory of Olev the Slant/Hilly, his father..." §A -(o)nasi * resti * sta : at : ulef * skaka * faþur * si * aka(b)a(r) * biy=k §B (i)s * leni * nu * byki * uhas * uniþikR * a * uikir * uas * a * ---r(a)----- * ---- * ratami §A Óneisi(?) raised the stone for Óleif Skakka/Skaga, his father <akabar> lived(?). §B <is> <leni> <nu> <byki> var óníðingr <a> <uikir> var <a> ... ... <ratami>. §A Onæisi(?) raised the stone for Olæif Skakka/Skaga, his father <akabar> built(?). §B <is> <leni> <nu> <byki> vas oniðingR <a> <uikir> vas <a> ... ... <ratami>.

Location: Sweden, Södermanland County, Strängnäs Municipality, Toresund.

Registration number: Sö 189.

How certain are we about this?

This is the Swedish National Heritage Board's archaeological registration of the stone itself – a physical and documented historical monument. The runic inscription itself is a contemporary primary source, but the interpretation of the text and its context may be uncertain, and dating is based on rune type and style rather than specific years.
tldr: An ancient runestone in the province of Södermanland tells the story of a person who erected the stone in memory of their father, named Olav. While the text on the stone is difficult to read completely, it provides clues about how people lived in the past.
lattlast:
The runestone is located in Toresund, within the municipality of Strängnäs. It's a large granite stone, over two meters tall. The stone features carved runes, which were the ancient alphabet used in Sweden in the past.

Part of the text on the stone can be read, and it tells us that a person named Onese erected the stone in memory of his father, Olav. Because these stones were erected long ago, they are important historical sources that help us understand the past.

Some parts of the inscription on the right side are difficult to decipher and have not been fully translated. This means that researchers cannot be completely certain about every word, but the stone remains an important historical artifact registered by the Swedish National Heritage Board (Riksantikvarieämbetet).
